One thing I&39;d add for anyone running a small business: most owners don&39;t actually need a multi-step agent for their first 90 days with AI. Content: Great explanation of the distinction between Generative AI, AI Agents, and Agentic AI systems. The key difference really lies in autonomy—Generative AI focuses on producing outputs, AI agents can take actions based on inputs, while Agentic AI systems go a step further by planning, reasoning, and executing multi-step goals with minimal human intervention. This progression clearly shows how we’re moving from passive models to more autonomous, goal-driven systems.

Content: Лучше дать совет которого не просили. У меня в жизни был ряд ужасных ситуаций и периодов которые навсегда кардинально изменили мою жизнь. Вокруг много хороших и любящих людей, но хоть бы кто-нибудь дал совет, что делать. Я бы многое за это отдал. А не давали потомучто сами не знают как в таких ситуациях себя вести - зелёные все с розовыми очками обычной жизни - ой, а что так бывает? Бывает блин, и не такое бывает! Теперь я сотни людей в таких состояниях знаю. Теперь видя какого-то человека в подобных ситуациях я готов трясти его за руки и говорить то что он не слушает, я знаю что это может спасти годы жизни и здоровье.Например видишь, человека у которого определенные проблемы со здоровьем, а ты 5 лет и 5 млн потратил чтобы найти истину и подходы в этом заболевании - не жди пока спросят, подойди и расскажи. Человек теряет отношения и очень переживает, не жди подойди скажи что его ждёт если он так будет переживать, это может убить его!Человек работает по 12 ч в сутки ради общего дела - подойди скажи ему что будет через 15 лет после такой работы и что ты через себя всё это пропустил.Этому не учат в школе, в школе учат всякой хрени, а как жить - нет.При этом по многим вопросам у большинства людей только один шанс в некоторых сферах жизни. Как сказал один мой мудрый врач - дурак ты ещё молодой, поэтому такой резвый, всё что нас не убивает не делает нас сильнее, оно делает нас инвалидами.Многие советы бесценны, но никто не знает у кого их спрашивать и в большинстве случаев не знает, что у него вообще проблема.На своих ошибках не все могут учиться, многие ошибки не пережить.

Content: Pretty dystopian and one dimensional view. I think people will look back and laugh a bit at this perspective in the future. Change has always been part of life, and for some reason we always think it is not applicable to us. Admittedly, it is happening faster now, and we need to adapt faster as well.No one knows what impact “automation” will have, but history has shown that it tends to open up new opportunities, including ones we do not even have the imagination to see yet. If you do not believe me, then drive your Car to your local Cinema right now, meanwhile you could also think about, what has enabled something like Kurzgesagt and others, to build their own company and hiring people.I would argude, that a better way to think about all this, is to ask what could exist instead, how would my seat transform going forward. People still want a handcrafted pizza. robots may take over the repetitive parts, but that does not instantly mean removing of people from the process. It can shift them toward things like crafting better experiences, experimenting with new recipes, becoming better hosts, or other roles we currently can not even imaging as part of “pizza making.”.When I hear these AI dystopian points of view, I often think about “If it ain’t mined, it’s grown.” The world is far more physical and grounded than we give credit for in our debates. Humans are build to adapt and constant being in motion, economics is about moving things and ideas around, that will never stop, humans will for sure pick something else up and move it somewhere.

Content: The Rise of the Machines offers a nuanced and visually engaging overview of how automation is reshaping economies, jobs, and even our concept of human value. It succeeds in demonstrating that technological change isn’t neutral — it redistributes power, labor, and opportunity. The section showing how machines can now perform not only manual but also cognitive tasks is particularly striking: it challenges the long-held assumption that creativity and judgment belong exclusively to humans.Still, the documentary sometimes leans toward techno-determinism — as if automation’s trajectory were inevitable rather than socially constructed. The reality is that technology doesn’t simply “advance”; it’s steered by political and corporate decisions about investment, regulation, and ownership. Who controls the machines, and who benefits from their productivity, are questions that deserve equal emphasis.The film also hints at universal basic income and lifelong education as possible solutions but doesn’t go far enough in examining the feasibility and ethical implications of these ideas. Can societies sustain dignity and meaning when traditional work declines? What replaces the sense of identity tied to labor?Overall, this documentary is both a warning and an invitation — not to fear machines, but to rethink what kind of future we are programming into them, and who gets to decide.

Content: ### *Chapter 1: Introduction to Cloud Computing**What is the Cloud?* [1, 2]* To understand the cloud, it helps to look at how organizations managed their applications 10-20 years ago [2].* Companies used to buy physical servers from providers like IBM or HP to run their applications [2].* They would store and manage these servers in their own **data centers**, which are physical locations that house servers and networking equipment, complete with temperature controls and wiring [2, 3].*The Problem with Physical Servers* [3, 4]* Physical servers are very expensive and powerful, often coming with high configurations like 100 GB of RAM and 100 CPU cores [3].* If a company deployed a single application that only used 1 GB of RAM and 1 CPU, the remaining 99% of the server&39;s resources were wasted [4].* This inefficiency was very costly, as buying a new server was required for each new application [4].*The Solution: Virtualization* [4, 5]* To solve the problem of wasted resources, the concept of *virtualization* was introduced [4].* Virtualization allows you to create multiple *virtual servers (or virtual machines)* on a single physical server [4].* This way, instead of buying 15 separate servers for 15 applications, a company could buy one powerful server and run all 15 applications on 15 virtual machines on that single piece of hardware [4, 5].* This concept is what makes "the cloud" possible. A developer can request a virtual machine and get access to it via an IP address without knowing where the physical server is actually located [5, 6].### *Chapter 2: Public Cloud vs. Private Cloud**What is a Private Cloud?* [6, 7]* When an organization builds and manages its own cloud infrastructure using virtualization within its own data centers, it is called a *private cloud* [6].* This setup is "private" because it is exclusively used by that single organization; no one from outside can access its resources [6, 7].* System administrators within the company manage this entire environment [6].* Companies in sectors like banking or those handling sensitive information often prefer private clouds, using platforms like OpenStack or VMware to build them [8].*What is a Public Cloud?* [7, 9, 10]* Companies like Amazon (AWS), Microsoft (Azure), and Google (GCP) saw an opportunity to offer cloud infrastructure as a service [7, 9].* They built massive data centers around the world and allow anyone to request computing resources (like virtual machines) from them [9].* This is called a *public cloud* because it is available to the general public. Anyone with an account can create and use resources [9, 10].* The cloud provider (e.g., AWS) manages the physical hardware and data centers, while the user has control over the virtual resources they create [10].*Key Differences:** *Management:* Private clouds are managed by the organization that uses them, while public clouds are managed by third-party providers like AWS, Microsoft, and Google [10].* *Security Concerns:* Some may feel public clouds are less secure because the infrastructure is shared. However, public clouds offer tools like *Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs)* that allow you to create isolated, secure sections within the public cloud for your resources [8, 10].### *Chapter 3: Why is the Public Cloud So Popular?*The popularity of public clouds like AWS is driven by two main factors [11]:1. *Reduced Maintenance Overhead (The Biggest Reason)* * For startups and mid-sized companies, building and maintaining a data center is a huge burden [11]. It requires a dedicated team of experts to manage servers, networking, security, and power 24/7 [11, 12]. * The public cloud eliminates this overhead. Companies can simply create an account and start using resources immediately, allowing them to focus on building their products instead of managing infrastructure [12].2. *Cost (Pay-as-you-go Model)* * Instead of making a massive upfront investment in servers and equipment, public cloud services operate on a *"pay-as-you-go"* model [8, 11]. * This means you only pay for the resources you actually use, which is much more affordable for startups and growing businesses [8].### *Chapter 4: Why is AWS the Most Popular Cloud Platform?** **First-Mover Advantage**: AWS was the pioneer in the public cloud market [13]. They were the first to see the opportunity and successfully create a cloud platform, so many companies began their cloud journey with AWS [13, 14].* **Large Market Share**: Because they started first, AWS has the largest share of the cloud market, followed by Azure and GCP [14].* **More Job Opportunities**: A larger market share means more companies use AWS, which in turn leads to a higher demand for engineers with AWS skills [14, 15]. This makes AWS an ideal platform to learn for anyone starting their cloud career [15].* **Vast Service Offerings**: AWS started with a few services like virtual machines (EC2) and storage, but today it offers over 200 services [12, 16]. They continuously add new services based on market trends, such as managed Kubernetes clusters, to simplify complex setups for their users [16].### *Chapter 5: Are Companies Moving Away from the Cloud?** You may have heard of a trend called *"cloud repatriation,"* where some companies move from the public cloud back to their own private cloud or on-premises data centers [17].* This is true, but it&39;s happening on a very small scale—only about *1-2% of users* are moving back [17].* The reasons for moving back can include security concerns or failing to achieve expected cost savings [18].* However, the overall trend of companies moving to the public cloud continues to grow, especially for startups and mid-sized businesses that cannot afford the cost and maintenance of their own data centers [18, 19]. Therefore, it is still a great time to learn about cloud platforms [17].### *Chapter 6: How to Create an AWS Account*Here is a step-by-step guide to creating your own AWS account to use for learning [19]:1. *Start the Process**: Go to your browser and search for "AWS sign in" [19]. On the sign-in page, click on **"Create new AWS account"* [20].2. **Provide Email and Account Name**: Enter your email address (this will be your "root user" email) and choose an AWS account name. The account name can be changed later [20].3. **Verify Your Email**: AWS will send a verification code to your email. Enter that code to proceed [20, 21].4. **Create a Password**: Set a strong password for your account [21].5. **Enter Personal Information**: * Select *"Personal"* for the account type [21]. * Provide your full name, phone number, country, and address [21, 22]. This information is for billing purposes and doesn&39;t have to be 100% precise, but basic validations (like phone number length) are checked [22].6. **Provide Payment Information**: * You must provide a valid *credit or debit card* [22]. This is for identity verification to prevent spam and ensure you are a legitimate user [23]. * AWS will make a small temporary charge (e.g., ₹2 in India or $1 in the US) to validate the card, which will be refunded [23, 24]. * *Important**: AWS will **not* automatically deduct money from your card if you incur charges. You will receive a bill, and if you don&39;t pay it, your account will be suspended [23, 25].7. **Final Steps**: After your payment details are verified, you will complete a few more simple steps (4 and 5) to finalize your account setup [26].Once your account is created, you can use it to follow along with the video series and learn AWS. Be mindful of the free tier limits to avoid unexpected charges [25].

Content: This video, "Day-0 | Course Details | Free AWS Zero to Hero Course for DevOps Engineers," introduces a 30-day "AWS Zero to Hero" course specifically designed for DevOps engineers. The course starts on June 25th (0:53). Here&39;s a breakdown of what the course will cover: Course Introduction (1:51): The course will begin with an introduction to AWS, covering private vs. public cloud, reasons for adopting cloud services, and how to create and navigate an AWS account (1:51-2:32). Core AWS Services (2:50-7:03): Participants will learn about essential AWS services such as Identity and Access Management (IAM) (3:07), EC2 instances (3:52), Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) and networking concepts (4:43), AWS security (5:23), and AWS Route 53 (DNS) (6:37). Practical Projects (7:06-8:06): Day 7 will feature a practical project that integrates the learned concepts of IAM, EC2, networking, security, and Route 53 to simulate a real-world application deployment. Storage and Infrastructure as Code (8:17-11:00): The course will delve into AWS S3 for storage and static website hosting (8:17) and AWS CloudFormation for infrastructure as code (9:37), including a live project (10:15). The video also mentions a brief session on AWS CLI (11:00). CI/CD Pipeline on AWS (12:11-13:22): Days 11-14 are dedicated to setting up a complete CI/CD pipeline using AWS services: AWS CodeCommit (12:30) AWS CodePipeline (12:36) AWS CodeBuild (12:43) AWS CodeDeploy (12:51) Monitoring, Serverless & Logging (13:39-16:09): The course will cover AWS CloudWatch for monitoring (13:54), AWS Lambda for serverless computing (14:14), and a project combining CloudWatch and Lambda for an event-driven workflow (14:48). It will also touch upon AWS CloudTrail for logging (15:27). Databases, Containers & Scaling (16:14-20:16): Topics include AWS DynamoDB for DevOps engineering (16:14), AWS ECS and EKS for container orchestration with a focus on EKS (17:00), AWS Systems Manager for secrets management (18:20), AWS Auto Scaling (18:47), AWS RDS (relational database service) (19:27), and AWS Elastic Load Balancer (19:46). Cloud Migration & Best Practices (20:32-21:51): The course concludes with cloud migration strategies and tools (20:32) and best practices for AWS jobs (21:10), including interview preparation and additional resources. The instructor emphasizes practical job readiness over certification (23:03). Additional Resources (22:15-22:44): The instructor will provide prior resources for complex topics like containers on his channel to ensure a smooth learning experience. The GitHub repository for the course will be continuously updated (1:15, 23:32).

Content: The meaning of imperative / declarative is the opposite of what&39;s mentioned in this course - declarative means you tell it the desired result / you declare what you want without having to specify the specific steps, while imperative means you tell it the exact steps you want to be executed. CDK is imperative meaning you have more granular control, but it&39;s more verbose, and CFN is declarative meaning you just say what you want and the rest is filled in.

Content: I’ve spent so much time searching for quality data engineering course - free and paid,but I’ve been disappointed every time. Most of what’s out there is just hype, with little to no real content. They scratch the surface, teach the bare minimum, and leave you feeling like you’ve been misled. It’s so frustrating to see how education is being commercialized, offering false promises and unrealistic expectations. I’ve been waiting for someone to break this cycle, someone who genuinely cares about teaching and sharing deep, practical knowledge. A true mentor, like the legends Corey Schafer, Kunal, or Durga, who inspire through their passion and depth of understanding. And then your video dropped. It’s different—it feels real, valuable, and impactful. This is what the data engineering community has been waiting for, and I know people will see that too. The Data Engineer Legend has arrived, and your work is going to make a lasting difference. Thank you for doing what so many others haven’t. 😊

Content: at 1:32:16 To make the Pie chart Display the real Percentages of each State taking in Consideration the whole amount of Emissions from all the state we can just calculate the percentage of each state and acquire it with it, we can do it just like thisselect state_abbr, SUM(cast(replace(`GHG emissions mtons CO2e`, &39;,&39;, &39;&39;) as double)) AS Total_Emissions, 100 * SUM(cast(replace(`GHG emissions mtons CO2e`, &39;,&39;, &39;&39;) as double)) / (SELECT SUM(cast(replace(`GHG emissions mtons CO2e`, &39;,&39;, &39;&39;) as double)) FROM emissions_data WHERE state_abbr IS NOT NULL) AS Emission_Percentage from emissions_data group by state_abbr order by Total_Emissions desc limit 10 then what we have to do is in the Label we its content to Field and choose Emission_Percentagethank you for your efforts sir 🙏🏻🙏🏻

Content: At 2:45:00 i see you had a problem with the %fs when created a directorie. I think I know what happened. The %fs interacts with the Databricks File System (DBFS). The DBFS is a distributed file system that acts as an abstraction layer over your cloud object storage (the things you can see in unity catalog). The %fs interacts with that, and not with the file system of the workspace. To interact with workspace file system you should use %sh or some program language like python (that interacts with both, DBFS and Workspace Files). When you created that directorie you had created not in your "/Workpace/.../folder", but in "dbfs:/Workspace/.../folder"Furthermore, I would like to express my immense gratitude for the content. I am from Brazil and my exam is tomorrow. This has been an excellent review.

Content: Cleared my Databricks Data Engineer Associate exam today! This course turned out to be incredibly helpful, it covered several areas I had initially overlooked. I also went through the official Databricks documentation and completed about 13 practice tests from Skill-cert-pro, which I found extremely useful and very close to the real exam. Around 80% of the questions felt similar, with many appearing word-for-word. The exam included a lot of scenario-based questions focused on topics like Delta Lake, DataFrame operations, Databricks SQL, and job scheduling, so having a strong understanding of these areas is essential. Some questions were quite detailed, so time management is important. Overall, this 7 hour course + Skill-cert-pro mock tests were all I needed to clear the exam.Good luck to everyone preparing

Content: 🎯 Key Takeaways for quick navigation:01:36 🚀 Databricks offers managed Spark services along with other tools like Delta Lake and MLflow, providing options for data processing and model deployment.03:56 🏠 Databricks and Snowflake both promote the concept of data lake houses, combining data warehouse and data lake functionalities, but with different emphases on use cases.05:37 🛠️ Key components of Databricks include workspaces, notebooks, tables, clusters, jobs, and libraries, providing an integrated environment for data processing and analysis.09:33 📊 Databricks simplifies the transition from notebooks to production by allowing users to create jobs directly from their notebooks, enabling seamless integration and scheduling.11:13 🌟 Databricks facilitates easier productionization of data science workflows compared to alternatives like Snowflake, with integrated features like job creation and version control.Made with HARPA AI

Content: Very impressive, Darshil! I really liked the structure—clear, logical, and unfolding the content one layer at a time.While the data space today is often described as a MAD landscape, the fundamentals still remain the same. Tools may evolve, new features will emerge, and the ecosystem will continue to shift—but with strong fundamentals, the journey becomes much easier.For learners: Having spent nearly 20 years in the industry, I can say this with some confidence and experience 😉. As Darshil rightly pointed out, don’t chase tools—they are simply a means to an end. The landscape changes so rapidly that by the time we finish talking, someone somewhere has already introduced and marketed the next “groundbreaking” solution. Don’t get distracted. Focus on the principles and keep learning as change is the only constant.

Content: If you are a linux user and getting database connection error host.docker.internal. Add this under dbt on docker compsoe file extra_hosts: - "host.docker.internal:host-gateway"Also if still getting problem about connection from dbt to database change DBT_Profile: custom_postgresAnd add extra volume paths under destination source postgres.This was a 1 month journey for me to solve this i hope this helps anyone

Content: This one took me a long time to figure out but if you &39;pg_isready&39; subprocess isn&39;t retrying, There&39;s potentially a small bug in the code that may be specific to linux when checking for "accepting_connections" in result.stdout.strip().On PopOS! the output is "accepting connections" with no underscore. The fix is to change the if statement to: if "accepting_connections" in result.stdout or "accepting connections" in result.stdout:

Content: Data Engineer&39;s definition and responsibilities probably depends a lot on company, but for me there are two missing items on this list:- orchestration - either I didn&39;t notice or you missed this one. You can&39;t really be a good, even entry level, Data Engineer, if you don&39;t understand how to schedule your jobs. Not everyone uses Databricks, but everyone is using Airflow, Dagster or Prefect. I would strongly recomment to learn Airflow (just use Astronomer&39;s Astro CLI locally) due to it&39;s market share.- IaaC - This might be only my experience, but whenever you work with Cloud, you almost never (except for dev environment) set it up manually. And industry standard for IaaC is still Terraform. I wouldn&39;t expect new hire or junior to write a module, but they at the very least understand the concepts of resource definitions, state files, and how terraform instantiates cloud resources.

Content: നിങ്ങൾ നൽകിയ വീഡിയോ 2026-ലെ സെർച്ച് എഞ്ചിൻ ഒപ്റ്റിമൈസേഷൻ (SEO) സംബന്ധിച്ച ഒരു സമ്പൂർണ്ണ കോഴ്‌സാണ്. ഈ വീഡിയോയിലെ പ്രധാന പോയിന്റുകൾ താഴെ പറയുന്നവയാണ്:​1. എന്താണ് SEO? [03:53]​ഗൂഗിൾ പോലുള്ള സെർച്ച് എഞ്ചിനുകളിൽ നിങ്ങളുടെ വെബ്‌സൈറ്റ് മുൻനിരയിൽ എത്തിക്കുന്നതിനായി ചെയ്യുന്ന പ്രക്രിയയാണിത്. ഇതിലൂടെ വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിലേക്ക് കൂടുതൽ വിസിറ്റേഴ്സിനെ എത്തിക്കാൻ സാധിക്കും.​2. SEO-യുടെ പ്രധാന മൂന്ന് തൂണുകൾ (Three Pillars of SEO) [23:47]​On-page SEO: വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിലെ ഉള്ളടക്കം (Content), കീവേഡുകൾ, ടൈറ്റിൽ ടാഗുകൾ എന്നിവ ഒപ്റ്റിമൈസ് ചെയ്യുന്നത്. [24:29]​Off-page SEO: മറ്റു വിശ്വസനീയമായ സൈറ്റുകളിൽ നിന്ന് ബാക്ക്‌ലിങ്കുകൾ (Backlinks) വഴി വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിന്റെ വിശ്വാസ്യത വർദ്ധിപ്പിക്കുന്നത്. [01:00:15]​Technical SEO: വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിന്റെ വേഗത, മൊബൈൽ ഫ്രണ്ട്ലിനസ്, സെക്യൂരിറ്റി (HTTPS) എന്നിവ മെച്ചപ്പെടുത്തുന്നത്. [01:16:45]​3. സെർച്ച് എഞ്ചിൻ എങ്ങനെ പ്രവർത്തിക്കുന്നു? [14:22]​Crawling: ഗൂഗിളിന്റെ &39;സ്പൈഡറുകൾ&39; ഇന്റർനെറ്റിലെ വിവരങ്ങൾ കണ്ടെത്തുന്നു. [15:23]​Indexing: കണ്ടെത്തിയ വിവരങ്ങൾ ഗൂഗിളിന്റെ ഡാറ്റാബേസിൽ തരംതിരിച്ച് സൂക്ഷിക്കുന്നു. [16:18]​Ranking: ഒരാൾ സെർച്ച് ചെയ്യുമ്പോൾ ഏറ്റവും അനുയോജ്യമായ വിവരങ്ങൾ ആദ്യ പേജിൽ കാണിക്കുന്നു. [17:13]​4. കീവേഡ് റിസർച്ച് (Keyword Research) [01:49:44]​ആളുകൾ ഗൂഗിളിൽ എന്താണ് സെർച്ച് ചെയ്യുന്നത് എന്ന് കണ്ടെത്തുന്ന പ്രക്രിയയാണിത്.​Short-tail keywords: ഒന്ന് അല്ലെങ്കിൽ രണ്ട് വാക്കുകൾ മാത്രം (ഉദാ: &39;Car wash&39;). [01:56:01]​Long-tail keywords: മൂന്നോ അതിലധികമോ വാക്കുകൾ (ഉദാ: &39;Best car washing products for luxury cars&39;). ഇവയിൽ മത്സരം കുറവായതിനാൽ പെട്ടെന്ന് റാങ്ക് ചെയ്യാൻ സാധിക്കും. [01:56:44]​5. പുതിയ സാങ്കേതിക വിദ്യകൾ (AEO, GEO, SXO) [02:20:44]​AEO (Answer Engine Optimization): AI അസിസ്റ്റന്റുകൾക്കും വോയ്‌സ് സെർച്ചിനും മറുപടി നൽകാൻ പാകത്തിൽ ഉള്ളടക്കം തയ്യാറാക്കുന്നത്. [02:21:57]​GEO (Generative Engine Optimization): AI ജനറേറ്റ് ചെയ്യുന്ന ഉത്തരങ്ങളിൽ നിങ്ങളുടെ സൈറ്റ് ഉൾപ്പെടുത്തുന്നത്. [02:26:52]​SXO (Search Experience Optimization): ഉപയോക്താവിന് വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിൽ മികച്ച അനുഭവം നൽകുന്നത്. [02:31:22]​6. ഒഴിവാക്കേണ്ട തെറ്റുകൾ [02:47:07]​മറ്റുള്ളവരുടെ ഉള്ളടക്കം കോപ്പി ചെയ്യുക (Duplicate Content). [02:48:02]​മോശം ബാക്ക്‌ലിങ്കുകൾ പണം കൊടുത്ത് വാങ്ങുക. [02:47:45]​മൊബൈൽ ഉപയോക്താക്കളെ അവഗണിക്കുക. [02:47:32]​പെട്ടെന്ന് ഫലം കിട്ടുമെന്ന് പ്രതീക്ഷിക്കുക

Content: That whole section about topical authority and building one cluster at a time instead of publishing random AI content everywhere was probably the most valuable advice in the video for me. You can tell you’ve actually done this in real campaigns because most people online just say “publish more content” without explaining structure or funnel logic. Also loved the point about AI assistants favoring clear structure, authority, and citations because that lines up with a lot of what we’ve been seeing too. We recently started exploring LLM Authority Index to better understand which competitor sources AI models trust most during recommendation-style searches, and honestly the citation/share-of-presence insights were eye-opening. Feels like AI visibility is becoming its own competitive layer on top of traditional SEO now. Super refreshing seeing someone connect SEO, AI visibility, funnels, and buyer intent together this clearly.

Content: 5:54 WRONG! It was the addition of the video html element support that YouTube and many others were waiting for, not Flash, which COULD NOT play video. I remember the month that it happened. No one else saw the new HTML element as anything more than a new tool in a tool box and were grateful for finally being able to add video to what they were already doing. Very few people understood that it was also the beginning of streaming. 6 to 10 months later, it seemed like the most obvious thing, and it also felt like they had already cornered the market.

Content: As a founder I completely disagree with the argument made about control. A founder is the person with the vision and drive to push the company forward. Its how companies grow in the first place. Corporate VCs and investors are box tickers, not passionate company builders. They want their return and they dont see long term like founders do. They can also be easily swayed by things they dont really understand about the business.Its important a founder retains control because a fpunder can make the tough decisions a board might not like, but save the company long term.Owning 20% of a 100m company is obviously financially better than 100% of a 1m company, but thats minimizing the value founders and their brand, drive and vision provide.

Content: Software as a Service (SaaS) Explained in 5 mins TLDR: Software as a Service (SaaS) is a cloud computing model that provides cost-efficient, scalable, and secure access to standardized software applications that are automatically updated for users. 00:00 💻 SaaS delivers software and applications over the internet via a subscription model, eliminating the need for users to manage, install, and upgrade software themselves, providing productivity apps and business operations. 01:34 💻 SaaS is the highest level of abstraction in cloud computing, where the provider maintains all layers of the IT stack. 01:47 👀 Users access SaaS applications within a multi-tenant architecture where multiple users securely store their data in the same hosted environment with many benefits. 02:32 💻 SaaS is cost-efficient and scalable because cloud service providers maintain and manage the application, eliminating IT overhead costs and allowing for subscription-based models with included support. 03:24 💻 SaaS applications provide the benefit of scalability and accessibility from anywhere with just a web browser and internet connectivity. 03:49 💻 SaaS is a highly secure solution that requires no installation or plugins and can be accessed from anywhere, but there are some instances where data must be kept in the same location as the user due to GDPR. 04:21 💻 SaaS automatically upgrades software versions, eliminating the need for manual updates and version control issues. 04:45 💻 Software as a Service (SaaS) eliminates compatibility issues and manual updates by providing a standardized version of software that is automatically updated for users.
Author: @AceCPAsBookkeeping

Content: DT is not Computerization, not sub-optimization of departments, but Optimization of the whole organization, all departments, all work processes. Business as a whole must have Objectives and Strategies to reach objectives. IT, business processes, employee skills, finance, .. are just tools to produce manageable plans to implement risky organization strategies to make customers, suppliers and partners happy, raising revenues while cutting internal costs, while the market regulations, customers, suppliers, and partners are also changing. This is a gamble that CEO and BOD are playing so deep industrial experience and willingness to intelligently choose your strategies are the key factors. Objectives and Strategies must be periodically reviewed and changed to reflect market reality.Tools like Business Model Canvas and BSC are appropriate.It is so rare that speakers talked about what is not DT. Thanks for your clearer presentations.

Content: Very basic and very poor. You have missed the point and the understanding of what Digital Transformation is all about. Digital transformation is the business journey into the new digital economy. Businesses need to focus on 4 main areas and they usually start with ‘Process Transformation’, which brings about opportunities to enhance one’s ‘Business Model’ that enable new powerful differentiators. They also need to be aware of ‘Domain transformation’ ultimately enabling they to compete in new segments never thought possible. But all of this can only be possible if they realise the ‘organisational/cultural’ transformation requirements.In my experience the main problems I see is that businesses just focus on Process Transformation as it improves efficiency ultimately improving profit, and then they stop. The word Digital is VERY important as people need to understand the different types of data. A business that creates an invoice as a pdf hasn’t really thought about the end to end process as that pdf document usually needs a human on the other side to interpret it, or they use document AI. You can scan a document which makes it hard for processes down the line to interpret it or you can digitize a document whereby other applications can access the data.Digital Transformation is all about end to end seamless processes, today most business processes are human to human, digital Transformation will enables you to evolve these processes to human to machine and in some cases machine to machine. To achieve this businesses will need to really understand their data and how to leverage it through every part of their business to maximise shareholder value.

Content: So, it sounds like what he&39;s saying is that we have been doing Digital Transformation all along??? This is really not a useful presentation, nor does it describe what the recent trend to Digital transformation is actually trying to achieve. The key thing in Digital Transformation is to focus on the Customer Journey and to use the data that you collect (or should collect) to provide insight to improve the Customer Experience. It is not about process, or efficiency, that is what we have been doing for the last 40 years and not being particularly amazing at! It&39;s about creating new business models to help you fulfil the customer&39;s needs, outside your normal core business. This is achieved looking at the Customer Journey from the customer perspective, not the business perspective. The buzzword here is to look from the Outside In rather than from the Inside Out. I hope people will not start to do &39;Digital Transformations&39; based on Eric&39;s consultancy, as it will keep you firmly in the 1990s and struggling to achieve any value. He&39;s right about one thing, it&39;s not just about technology, people have to change their mindsets, but that is hard when the consultant hasn&39;t changed his.

Content: Vous utilisez ChatGPT pour votre activité immobilière ? Alors voici l’erreur que beaucoup de professionnels commettent encore. Ils demandent simplement : "Rédige-moi une annonce immobilière." ou "Fais-moi un post LinkedIn." Résultat : un texte générique, peu différenciant et rarement adapté à leur cible. L’IA n’est pas magique. La qualité du résultat dépend directement de la qualité de la demande. Par exemple, au lieu de demander : ❌ "Rédige une annonce immobilière." Essayez : ✅ "Rédige une annonce immobilière pour une maison familiale de 120 m² avec jardin, située dans un secteur recherché. Content: 🚀💻 **Aprendizaje Inteligente en Programación: Evidencia Científica para la Educación del Futuro** La Inteligencia Artificial está transformando la enseñanza de la programación. Este estudio presenta una revisión sistemática de la literatura que analiza cómo las tecnologías inteligentes están mejorando el aprendizaje de la programación en distintos niveles educativos. 🔍 **Hallazgos principales:** ✔️ **Problema abordado:** La enseñanza de la programación enfrenta desafíos relacionados con la personalización del aprendizaje, la retroalimentación oportuna y el desarrollo de habilidades de resolución de problemas. Las tecnologías basadas en IA surgen como una alternativa prometedora para enfrentar estas limitaciones. ✔️ **Metodología rigurosa:** Mediante el protocolo PRISMA se analizaron **125 estudios científicos** provenientes de **Scopus** e **IEEE Xplore**, seleccionando finalmente **22 investigaciones de alta calidad** publicadas entre 2022 y 2026. ✔️ **Resultados destacados:** 🤖 Los **Sistemas Tutores Inteligentes (ITS)**, los **Modelos de Lenguaje de Gran Escala (LLMs)** como ChatGPT, las plataformas de aprendizaje adaptativo y los entornos gamificados fueron las tecnologías más utilizadas. 📈 Los estudios reportan mejoras significativas en: • Rendimiento académico • Resolución de problemas • Depuración de código • Pensamiento computacional • Motivación y compromiso estudiantil ⚠️ **Desafíos identificados:** Persisten preocupaciones relacionadas con la dependencia excesiva de herramientas de IA, respuestas incorrectas generadas por los modelos, sesgos algorítmicos y la escasez de estudios de largo plazo. 💡 **Conclusión principal:** La Inteligencia Artificial tiene el potencial de revolucionar la educación en programación, pero sus mejores resultados se alcanzan cuando complementa la labor docente y favorece experiencias de aprendizaje más personalizadas, adaptativas y centradas en el estudiante. 📚 Revisión Sistemática de Literatura 🎓 Educación en Programación • Inteligencia Artificial • ChatGPT • Sistemas Tutores Inteligentes • Aprendizaje Adaptativo InteligenciaArtificial Educacion Programacion ChatGPT LLM SmartLearning TecnologiaEduca

Content: GenUI shows the result. But where does the agent get the data? 🔌 Meet MCP — the Model Context Protocol. The clean mental model: MCP fetches and acts; GenUI builds the interface on top. Agent calls a tool (search_flights) → gets structured JSON → emits an A2UI blueprint (flight cards + Select) → your native app renders it → tap Select → book_flight → loop. Every action is an MCP call; every screen is a GenUI surface. Why now: donated to the Linux Foundation (Dec 2025), ~10k public servers, early-majority adoption. Content: Your store is closed. The sale isn't. Meta just rolled out Business Agent at Cannes Lions: an AI that replies, recommends products and closes the sale 24/7 inside WhatsApp, Instagram and Messenger DMs. Over 1M businesses are already running it. The shift is real: the highest-intent moment is a customer sliding into your DMs at midnight, and most businesses miss it because nobody is awake to answer. An always-on agent catches that intent, answers the question, and sends the checkout link before the buyer cools off. How to use it without losing your brand voice: 1. Feed it your real catalogue, prices, shipping and returns so answers are accurate. 2. Set the tone and the hard rules (what it can discount, when to hand off to a human). 3. Give it one job per conversation: answer, recommend, then close with a link. 4. Review the transcripts weekly and tighten the gaps. Treat the agent as a junior closer you train, not a black box you trust blindly. Would you let an AI close sales in your DMs? Content: Insurance claims are not just an operations process. They are where trust is won or lost. When claims move through emails, spreadsheets, old portals, and manual reviews, the business creates hidden risk. That risk shows up as: • slower decisions • poor customer updates • duplicate work • missed recovery opportunities • overpayment • higher support volume • lower retention This is called claims leakage. And it is one of the quietest profit drains in insurance. PwC found that improving claims leakage can often create 5–10% savings. JD Power also found that customers who rate their digital claims experience as poor or just okay are far more likely to leave or not renew. The lesson is simple: Claims modernization is not just about speed. It is about better data, cleaner workflows, smarter routing, clearer updates, and stronger trust. AI can help. Automation can help. Better SaaS platforms can help. But only when the system is built around real insurance workflows. At Zenveus, we help insurance companies build custom software, AI workflows, SaaS platforms, customer portals, and automation systems that make operations faster, cleaner, and easier to scale. Content: Most IAM reviews focus on individual permissions. But attackers don't think that way. They look for combinations. A permission that appears harmless on its own can become dangerous when combined with another permission elsewhere in the environment. Consider examples like: • KMS Decrypt + S3 Read • Secrets Manager Access + Lambda Update • CloudTrail Stop Logging + IAM Modification • S3 Policy Changes + Bucket Read/Write Individually, these permissions may not trigger major concern. Together, they can create a direct path to data exposure, privilege escalation, or reduced visibility during an attack. This is one of the biggest challenges in cloud security today. Most tools identify individual findings. Few analyze how permissions interact across identities, resources, and services to create hidden attack paths. Securitain was built to address exactly this problem. By analyzing IAM permissions, trust relationships, resource access, and toxic permission combinations, Securitain helps AWS teams uncover risks that may remain hidden in traditional security reviews. Because the real risk is often not a single permission. It's the combination. 🔐 Detect hidden attack paths 🔐 Identify toxic permission combinations 🔐 Prioritize risks based on real impact 🔐 Improve AWS IAM security posture Securitain by Cloudain Making cloud security findings easier to understand, prioritize, and remediate. Content: Innovation Or Better Marketing? A new AI infrastructure battle is heating up. Databricks recently unveiled its LTAP architecture, calling it the first system to combine transactional and analytical databases into a single copy. SingleStore wasn't impressed. CEO Raj Verma quickly pushed back, arguing that SingleStore has offered the same capability for years and already serves hundreds of customers using it. The company's response was blunt: "We didn't announce it. We shipped it, seven years ago." SingleStore also challenged Databricks' open-source narrative, claiming the architecture creates deeper customer lock-in. Here's what most people miss: In tech, winning isn't always about inventing first. It's often about owning the narrative. Many breakthrough technologies become famous only when a larger player enters the market. Real question: What matters more in technology: being first to build it, or being the company that convinces the world it matters? GenLeaders Databricks SingleStore AIInfrastructure DataInfrastructure

Content: $6.9 billion in annualized revenue and it’s actually speeding up. Databricks just announced its annualized revenue hit $6.9B, up more than 80% year-over-year from $5.4B last quarter. CEO Ali Ghodsi shared the milestone at the company’s Data and AI Summit. The AI product line alone now runs at $1.7B, up from $1.4B just four months earlier, with net revenue retention holding above 140%. But there’s a twist: gross margins slipped to ~74% from above 80% as AI agents fire off far more queries and compute, and Ghodsi expects them to fall further. At a $134B valuation, Databricks is now worth more than public rival Snowflake. Is shrinking margins the real cost of the agentic AI boom? 👇 Databricks AI enterpriseAI dataanalytics AliGhodsi AIagents

Content: "AGI is here today. Just not at work." Content: This is the SQL window function cheat sheet I wish people memorized. Not every function is a random trick. They fall into groups. Number rows: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK. Look around: LAG, LEAD. Calculate without collapsing: SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX. Control the window: PARTITION BY, ORDER BY, and frames. That is the real interview pattern. If you can say which group, which order, and which nearby rows matter, most window function questions become simple. Save this before your next SQL interview or analytics PR. Which one still trips you up? HackProduct SQL DataEngineering AnalyticsEngineering InterviewPrep

Content: ¿Tu negocio crea valor con sus datos o solo quema dinero? 📊🔥 La mayoría de las empresas no sufren por falta de datos. Tienen ventas, clientes, inventario y marketing registrándose 24/7. El verdadero problema es la falta de dirección. He visto demasiados equipos invertir miles de dólares en dashboards complejos, reportes infinitos y herramientas de analítica que al final nadie usa para tomar decisiones. Datos que se acumulan en un data lake o en hojas de cálculo, pero que jamás se traducen en acción. Eso no es gestión de datos. Eso es gasto de datos. 💸 Y ojo, esto se vuelve crítico si tienes en la mira la automatización o la Inteligencia Artificial. El 90% de los proyectos de IA y Machine Learning fracasan por lo mismo: no tener una infraestructura de datos sólida detrás. Sin esa base estructurada, ningún algoritmo (por más avanzado o costoso que sea) te va a dar resultados confiables. Basura entra, basura sale. Un negocio que realmente sabe monetizar y aprovechar su información, responde estas 3 preguntas antes de tocar una sola línea de código o comprar un software: 1️⃣ ¿Qué decisión de negocio quiero mejorar con esta información? 2️⃣ ¿Quién la va a usar en el día a día y para qué exactamente? 3️⃣ ¿Cómo voy a medir el impacto financiero ocn esa decisión? Sin estas respuestas claras, cualquier inversión en infraestructura o analítica es solo ruido caro. 👉 Desliza en el carrusel para ver la diferencia exacta entre los datos que generan ROI y los que solo generan costos en la nube, y los pasos clave para empezar a ordenar tu casa digital. 📩 ¿Quieres auditar cómo están tus datos hoy y dónde está la oportunidad real de optimización en tu negocio? Content: Two months in. Here is what building with AI actually looks like for one of our engineers. The technical work was real from week one. A Snowflake MCP Gateway deployed on Azure. A product query agent for a retail client where the debugging never really stopped. An order processing pipeline with human review queues, business rule validation, and a conversational interface operators could use in plain language. Then came the work she did not expect. Co-facilitating AI training sessions for enterprise teams. Building a product-specific agent. Now moving into a finance agent where the data is more sensitive, the rules are stricter, and the margin for error is smaller. The lesson that stuck. The model is almost never where it breaks. It breaks at the context. The schema. The structure of what you hand the system before it is asked to do anything. Pratiksha came in from a marketing analytics background. It turned out to be the thing that made her useful fastest. Knowing what a business team actually needs from a tool is not a soft skill in this work. It is the job. Building a system well and explaining it well are completely different skills. Follow to hear more such stories. Topics: AI engineering, production AI, AI agents, enterprise AI

Content: Trust is usually treated like a brand issue, but in modern marketing it is also tied closely to how well the business is organized behind the scenes. A company can have a good logo, a polished website, and strong visuals, but still create a weak customer experience if the systems behind the marketing are disconnected. You see this when an ad sends people to a page that does not match the offer, when an inquiry form does not trigger the right follow-up, when customer information sits in the wrong place, or when the sales conversation feels disconnected from what the marketing promised. Customers may not see the system behind the business, but they feel the result of it. They feel it when the response is slow, when the next step is unclear, when the message changes from one place to another, or when the business does not feel as organized after the inquiry as it did before. That is why marketing infrastructure matters. The website, CRM, email follow-up, lead handling, advertising, content, automation, and reporting should work together as one connected customer journey. When those pieces are aligned, the business has more control over what happens after someone shows interest. This is where modern marketing systems become valuable. They do not just help a business move faster. They help the business respond more consistently, manage leads more properly, and create a stronger experience from first impression to follow-up. For businesses that want to grow, the goal is not just to generate more attention. The goal is to make sure the system behind the attention is strong enough to support the customer when they are ready to move forward. Graphic direction: Create a LOGIC Marketing Systems static post with the headline “Trust Needs Infrastructure.” Use a modern connected-system visual showing Website, CRM, Email, Ads, Lead Follow-Up, and Reporting linked into one controlled customer journey. Keep the design clean, technical, premium, and business-friendly, with a polished systems architecture feel. Content: Digital Marketing (SEO, PPC & Lead Generation) A business owner told me last year that digital marketing does not work for his industry. I asked him what he had tried. He ran a few Facebook ads two years ago. Got some clicks. No conversions. Stopped. That was his entire data set. One short experiment, written off as proof. Meanwhile his direct competitor, same city, same industry, had spent eighteen months building a content and SEO strategy that now brings in 60% of their revenue from inbound leads. They did not get lucky. They got consistent. Here is what the data actually says about businesses that invest properly in digital marketing. SEO leads have a 14.6% close rate. Outbound leads average 1.7%. The same sales effort closes at nearly nine times the rate when the prospect found you first instead of the other way around. 70% of B2B buyers have already completed their research before they ever contact a vendor. If your business is not visible during that research phase, you do not lose the deal. You never enter the consideration set at all. Businesses that maintain a consistent content strategy generate 67% more leads per month than those that do not. Not from increasing ad spend. From expertise that works while they sleep. The businesses I see winning are rarely the ones with the single best product in their category. They are the ones prospects find first, trust fastest, and remember longest. Marketing is not a cost center sitting separately from the business. It is the mechanism that makes every other part of the business possible to sell. The founder who gave up after one failed ad campaign was not wrong that his first attempt did not work. He was wrong to conclude that meant the channel did not work. The question worth asking is not whether you can afford to invest properly in digital marketing. It is what it is quietly costing you, every single month, that you do not. DigitalMarketing SEO LeadGeneration PPC BusinessGrowth

In einer Zeit, in der wir mit einem einzigen Post Millionen Menschen erreichen können, reicht es für mich nicht mehr, nur schön auszusehen, schön zu wohnen, Produkte zu verkaufen oder dem nächsten Trend hinterherzulaufen. Denn jede Empfehlung ist eine Entscheidung. Jede Kooperation ist eine Entscheidung. Jedes Schweigen ist eine Entscheidung. Und ich glaube, wir dürfen uns öfter fragen: Würde ich diese Marke auch unterstützen, wenn niemand dafür bezahlen würde? Passt das zu meinen Werten? Mache ich die Welt damit ein kleines bisschen besser oder einfach nur lauter? Für mich bedeutet Social Media zu machen nicht, Menschen zum Kaufen zu bringen. Es bedeutet: Menschen zum Denken zu bringen. Zum Fühlen. Zum Hinterfragen. Zum Verbinden. Ich möchte keine Marken unterstützen, die Menschen ausbeuten. Ich möchte keine Kampagnen machen, für die ich meine Werte an der Garderobe abgebe. Ich möchte meine Stimme nutzen für mentale Gesundheit, Gleichberechtigung, Nachhaltigkeit, Inklusion und die Themen, die mir wirklich wichtig sind. Und nein, ich mache das nicht perfekt. Aber ich versuche es bewusst. Denn wir leben in einer Welt, die uns permanent sagt: Mehr kaufen. Mehr besitzen. Mehr optimieren. Während wir eigentlich viel mehr brauchen: Mehr Menschlichkeit. Mehr Gemeinschaft. Mehr Verantwortung. Vielleicht ist das die eigentliche Frage: Nicht, wie groß unsere Reichweite ist. Sondern wofür wir sie nutzen. 🤍 contentcreator influencermarketing mentalhealth nachhaltigkeit feminismus

Content: 🎙️ La Skwatt Box sur ! Nous avons eu le plaisir d'être invités à participer à l'émission "La France Bouge", animée par , dans les locaux de La Poste Pro à Strasbourg, pour présenter Skwatt et notre vision de la recharge des véhicules électriques. Avec l'essor des véhicules électriques, chaque bâtiment relié au réseau électrique devient potentiellement un point de recharge. Pourtant, le véritable défi n'est pas seulement de fournir de l'énergie, mais aussi de pouvoir contrôler, sécuriser et facturer simplement les recharges. C'est précisément la mission de Skwatt : proposer une alternative légère, économique et fabriquée en France aux bornes de recharge traditionnelles, spécialement pensée pour les gîtes, hôtels, campings, chambres d'hôtes et locations touristiques. Merci à Elisabeth Assayag, aux équipes d'Europe 1 et de La Poste Pro pour leur accueil et cette belle opportunité de mettre en lumière l'innovation française au service de la mobilité électrique. 🚗⚡🇫🇷 Europe1 LaFranceBouge ElisabethAssayag Innovation Startup MobilitéÉlectrique RechargeVE MadeInFrance Skwatt

Content: Being an employee was never really my thing. For years I’ve been trying to build something of my own, different attempts, different paths, different failures and successes. But things don’t always go the way we want. So I reached a point where I had to start a job again. I like the experience it brings, but the conditions? Not working for me at all. Shifts during weekends for a person like me who believes in work-life balance, who is living away from her hometown and is genuinely alive through her connections and friends, losing the chance of seeing any human being in person during a whole week was far away from my thoughts and expectations. Also the salary sucks and there is no perk and leave at all! But this is the fact of my life right now. I am not complaining. I am sharing this because I know I am not alone in this. Many people, especially founders, are living this double life. Building something meaningful on the side while paying their bills somewhere else. So don’t feel bad or embarrassed if you are experiencing the same, and don’t be surprised when you see a founder with an “Open to Work” badge on their LinkedIn profile. That’s not failure. That’s just the part nobody posts about. I chose this path consciously. Not because I had no options, but because building something meaningful takes time, and time needs stability. The job is the bridge. What I’m building is the destination. If you’re somewhere in this same messy in-between, building on the side, figuring it out, holding two lives at once, my DM is open. You don’t have to figure it out alone. solofounder solopreneur enterpreneur startup

Content: Insurance claims are not just an operations process. They are where trust is won or lost. When claims move through emails, spreadsheets, old portals, and manual reviews, the business creates hidden risk. That risk shows up as: • slower decisions • poor customer updates • duplicate work • missed recovery opportunities • overpayment • higher support volume • lower retention This is called claims leakage. And it is one of the quietest profit drains in insurance. PwC found that improving claims leakage can often create 5–10% savings. JD Power also found that customers who rate their digital claims experience as poor or just okay are far more likely to leave or not renew. The lesson is simple: Claims modernization is not just about speed. It is about better data, cleaner workflows, smarter routing, clearer updates, and stronger trust. AI can help. Automation can help. Better SaaS platforms can help. But only when the system is built around real insurance workflows. At Zenveus, we help insurance companies build custom software, AI workflows, SaaS platforms, customer portals, and automation systems that make operations faster, cleaner, and easier to scale. The future of insurance will belong to companies that make claims feel simple. insurance insurtech insurancetechnology claimsautomation aiininsurance insurancesoftware saas digitaltransformation insuranceclaims automation customsoftwaredevelopment zenveus
